name: master mexican black beans
instructions:
THREE TIERS OF INCREASING COMPLEXITY.

TIER 1 — BASIC MEXICAN BLACK BEANS (simple bean side, 5 min):
- Warm olive oil in a pan. Add minced garlic, bloom briefly.
- Add black beans (canned, with or without their liquid).
- Stir in dried thyme and dried oregano. Add coriander sparingly. Salt to taste.
- Cook through. Stir in a generous handful of fresh cilantro before serving (be liberal — black beans take cilantro well).

TIER 2 — ADOBO BLACK BEANS (cooked-down side, ~20 min):
Build on Tier 1, then:
- Stir in chipotle chiles in adobo sauce early in the cook.
- Add a splash of stock (vegetable stock is the right choice — see notes).
- Simmer down to a thickened side dish, NOT a soup. The liquid should mostly cook off; you want shiny, glossy beans that hold together, not a brothy stew.
- Finish with a splash of white vinegar or white wine vinegar for kick.
- Cilantro stirred in at the very end.

TIER 3 — ADOBO BLACK BEAN STEW (main, 45 min):
This is no longer a side — it's a main dish.
- FINELY SHRED THE SOFFRITO BASE: use a food processor to finely shred a combination of white onions, turnips, green bell peppers, celery, and garlic. Use any or all. The food processor turns this into a unified shredded vegetable base; knife-and-board chunks are too coarse here — the vegetables should dissolve into the stew.
- SAUTÉ THE BASE: in a heavy pot with olive oil, sauté the shredded soffrito until softened and slightly browned. This is the structural backbone of the stew.
- ADD THE CHIPOTLE: stir in chipotle chiles in adobo sauce.
- BUILD THE STEW: add black beans (at least 2 cans, or 1 can black + 1 can white), vegetable stock, oregano, thyme. Coarsely chop carrots and add them now (NOT into the soffrito — see notes). Simmer until heated through and thickened.
- FINISH: a splash of white vinegar or white wine vinegar. Adjust salt and black pepper. Stir in chopped cilantro.
- SERVE WITH: a dollop of greek yogurt or sour cream. Optional fresh thinly-sliced turnip or radish for crunch.
notes:
BLACK BEANS LOVE HERBS, VINEGAR, AND CORIANDER — NOT CUMIN AND DRIED CHILES: this is the structural rule that organizes all three tiers. Pinto beans want cumin and the dried chile family (ancho, pasilla, guajillo, california) and read best in a chili colorado / american-chili register. Black beans go in the opposite direction — they want fresh herbs (cilantro, dill, oregano, thyme), vinegar for brightness, and coriander as the warm-spice slot. Cumin in particular pulls black beans toward american chili territory and away from their own identity. Keep the two bean families on their own spice axes.

VEGETABLE STOCK OVER CHICKEN STOCK: another pinto-vs-black inversion. Pinto-based dishes (chile colorado, refried beans) prefer chicken stock — the meaty backbone reinforces the heavier chile-and-cumin direction. Black bean dishes prefer vegetable stock — it stays out of the herb/vinegar/coriander register's way and lets the beans and herbs lead. Chicken stock isn't wrong, but it's a downgrade here.

NO OTHER MEXICAN CHILIES OR CHILI POWDERS: in tiers 2 and 3, the chipotle in adobo carries ALL the chile work. Adding ancho, pasilla, guajillo, california, or any chili powder muddies the dish — those belong in chile colorado, not here. Stick to chipotle + adobo as the chile component.

NO TOMATOES (CLASHING): fresh, canned, and paste are all wrong. Tomato pulls the dish toward american chili and stops the adobo + soffrito framing from working — the dish ends up wanting cheddar cheese instead of greek yogurt, and what should be a clean herb-and-bean dish turns into a generic american chili with chipotle. Coriander, on the other hand, does NOT drag the dish to that bad place — it sits as a real seasoning option.

VINEGAR FOR KICK (TIERS 2+): white vinegar or white wine vinegar both work. A splash at the end brightens the dish — black beans + chipotle can read heavy without the acid pop. Red wine vinegar is too aggressive here.

FRESH HERBS LIBERALLY, CORIANDER SPARINGLY: in all three tiers, fresh cilantro should be applied with a generous hand at the end. Dill works too (especially in tier 1 simple-side mode). Dried oregano and thyme also go in heavy. Coriander is the one that wants restraint — its warmth is welcome but it can crowd out the fresh herbs if overdone.

=== TIER 1 NOTES ===

EXTREMELY SIMPLE — MOST INGREDIENTS OPTIONAL: tier 1 is the flex-around-what-you-have version. Black beans, olive oil, garlic, fresh cilantro, salt, pepper is the bare minimum. Thyme, oregano, and coriander are all expected but the dish is OK without any one of them on a given day.

=== TIER 2 NOTES ===

TIER 2 IS THE COOKED-DOWN SIDE — NOT A SOUP: the chipotle, stock, and vinegar bring complexity but the dish is still a side, not a stew. Cook the liquid down until the beans are glossy and the dish holds together when scooped. If it's still soupy at the end of the cook, simmer it down further.

TIER 2 PAIRINGS — FISH, RAW CABBAGE, RAW ONIONS, SALSA: the cooked-down adobo black beans are exceptionally good alongside fish (white fish or otherwise), raw shredded cabbage, raw thinly-sliced onions, and salsa. This is taqueria-plate territory — the beans are one component on a plate of fresh and grilled things, not the centerpiece.

=== TIER 3 NOTES ===

FINELY SHREDDED SOFFRITO IS THE TECHNIQUE: the food processor is doing real work here. A knife dice is too coarse — the vegetables stay discrete and the stew reads as 'beans with chunks' rather than 'beans in a unified savory base.' The processor reduces the soffrito to a paste-adjacent texture that dissolves into the stew during the sauté. Any combination of white onions, turnips, green bell peppers, celery, and garlic works; use what you have.

QUANTITY MATTERS — WORTH-THE-DRIVE THRESHOLD: tier 3 isn't worth making with one can of beans. You want at least 2 cans of black beans, or 1 can black + 1 can white. The volume of beans relative to the soffrito is what makes the stew feel substantial — under-bean it and the dish reads thin. White beans alongside black is a real variation, not a substitute.

HERBS ARE CREATIVE TERRITORY — BE HEAVY-HANDED: oregano and thyme are both robust herbs that black beans take well. Dill is also welcome. This isn't a slot with a rigid herb axis — pile them on.

DAIRY FINISH: greek yogurt or sour cream on top. Both work; greek yogurt is leaner and slightly tangier, sour cream is richer. The dairy is the right cooling element against the chipotle heat — not lime, not cream.

CARROTS — COARSE CHOP, INTO THE STEW (NOT INTO THE SOFFRITO): coarsely chop carrots and add them at the build-the-stew stage. Don't shred them with the soffrito base. The soffrito wants to be a unified savory paste that dissolves into the stew; shredded carrot adds sweetness to that base and throws the savory balance off. Chunky carrot in the stew itself reads as a vegetable component rather than as part of the aromatic backbone.

RADISHES: fresh, thinly sliced, scattered on top at serving for crunch. Same role as cabbage in tier 2 — a fresh raw component against the cooked stew.
